testingRequirements: Tests must validate user-facing behavior, not implementation details
Focus on what users actually do - integration tests over unit tests
Don't mock excessively - test real behavior where possible
Coverage metrics are meaningless - user flow coverage is everything

userStories.title: Add epic review run schema and orchestration records
acceptanceCriteria: A new epic review run record can be created for an epic with one or more ticket ids
The record stores the freeform steering prompt verbatim
The record distinguishes orchestration metadata from ticket-level findings/history
Existing review findings, comments, demo scripts, and workflow state remain untouched
The implementation is structured so ticket-level review actions can link back to the epic review run later
userStories.description:
Create the persistence layer for epic-scoped focused review runs.

## Goal
Add a new epic-level orchestration record that captures the review campaign without replacing ticket-level findings or comments.

## Scope
- Define schema/storage for an epic review run
- Store epic id, selected ticket ids, steering prompt, launch mode/provider, status, timestamps, and summary fields
- Add core create/read/update helpers or server-side access paths needed by the UI and launch flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new epic review run record can be created for an epic with one or more ticket ids
- The record stores the freeform steering prompt verbatim
- The record distinguishes orchestration metadata from ticket-level findings/history
- Existing review findings, comments, demo scripts, and workflow state remain untouched
- The implementation is structured so ticket-level review actions can link back to the epic review run later

## Notes
This is the foundation ticket. Do not collapse findings into the epic record; preserve ticket-level source of truth.

userStories.title: Add review-mode launch contract and focused prompt builder
acceptanceCriteria: Launch code can accept a review-mode input separate from the normal implementation launch path
The generated review prompt explicitly says to review only the selected ticket and not pick unrelated work
The prompt includes the freeform steering text when provided
The prompt still requires Brain Dump review workflow steps (findings, fixes, check-complete, demo where applicable)
The code path is clearly distinguishable from the default Ralph implementation prompt
userStories.description:
Introduce a dedicated review launch mode instead of reusing the generic implementation Ralph prompt.

## Goal
Allow the system to launch a ticket review with focused instructions and an optional steering prompt while still honoring the existing Brain Dump workflow.

## Scope
- Extend the launch contract to support a review mode/profile
- Add a review-specific prompt builder that includes selected ticket context and optional steering text
- Ensure the steering prompt is additive guidance and cannot override workflow constraints
- Avoid calling implementation-specific startup behavior when the intent is review-only

## Acceptance Criteria
- Launch code can accept a review-mode input separate from the normal implementation launch path
- The generated review prompt explicitly says to review only the selected ticket and not pick unrelated work
- The prompt includes the freeform steering text when provided
- The prompt still requires Brain Dump review workflow steps (findings, fixes, check-complete, demo where applicable)
- The code path is clearly distinguishable from the default Ralph implementation prompt

## Notes
Treat this as a profile/template system for now, not a generic arbitrary skill runner.

userStories.title: Build epic review modal with ticket selection and steering prompt
acceptanceCriteria: The epic detail UI lets the user choose one or more tickets to review
The UI includes a freeform textarea for review steering instructions
The launch action clearly communicates that this is a focused review flow, not a generic Ralph relaunch
The user can submit with no steering text or with custom steering text
Empty-state, loading, and launch-error behavior are handled cleanly
userStories.description:
Add the first-class epic review UI for selecting tickets and steering the review.

## Goal
Replace the misleading generic relaunch UX with a review-specific entry point that matches what the user expects.

## Scope
- Add or update the epic review modal
- Support selecting one or more tickets from the current epic
- Add a textarea labeled clearly for review steering/focus instructions
- Validate empty/invalid selection states and launch feedback

## Acceptance Criteria
- The epic detail UI lets the user choose one or more tickets to review
- The UI includes a freeform textarea for review steering instructions
- The launch action clearly communicates that this is a focused review flow, not a generic Ralph relaunch
- The user can submit with no steering text or with custom steering text
- Empty-state, loading, and launch-error behavior are handled cleanly

## Notes
Suggested UX copy can be simple, e.g. "How do you want to steer the review?"

userStories.title: Link epic review runs into ticket audit trail without replacing history
acceptanceCriteria: A ticket included in an epic review run shows auditable evidence that it was reviewed under that run
Existing ticket comments/findings are preserved and remain visible
New review artifacts can be associated back to the epic review run for traceability
Epic-level summary data can be derived or displayed without duplicating the entire ticket history
Human review/demo history remains compatible with the new review run layer
userStories.description:
Make the new epic review layer visible in the existing ticket-level audit trail.

## Goal
Keep all existing ticket comments, findings, and human-review artifacts intact while documenting that a ticket was part of an epic-focused review run.

## Scope
- Add linking/traceability from tickets to the originating epic review run
- Write ticket-visible comments or activity entries when a ticket is included in an epic review run
- Ensure findings and fixes created during the run can be traced back without replacing prior history
- Add epic-visible summary/read paths as needed for documentation and drill-down

## Acceptance Criteria
- A ticket included in an epic review run shows auditable evidence that it was reviewed under that run
- Existing ticket comments/findings are preserved and remain visible
- New review artifacts can be associated back to the epic review run for traceability
- Epic-level summary data can be derived or displayed without duplicating the entire ticket history
- Human review/demo history remains compatible with the new review run layer

## Notes
This ticket is about layering and traceability, not redefining the review source of truth.

userStories.title: Fan out multi-ticket review launches with isolated per-ticket execution
acceptanceCriteria: A single epic review request can include multiple selected tickets
Each selected ticket gets its own isolated review execution context
Ticket prompts and review artifacts do not bleed across tickets
The epic review run records per-ticket status so users can tell what launched and what finished/failed
The design supports future parallel execution cleanly, even if the first implementation executes sequentially under the hood
userStories.description:
Support launching focused review across multiple selected tickets while keeping each review run isolated.

## Goal
Allow the user to kick off a single epic review request that can target multiple tickets, while ensuring each ticket still has clean context, state, and auditability.

## Scope
- Accept multiple selected ticket ids in the epic review launch flow
- Fan out into one ticket-scoped review execution per selected ticket
- Keep prompts, state, and findings isolated per ticket
- Support sequential or parallel execution without mixing ticket context

## Acceptance Criteria
- A single epic review request can include multiple selected tickets
- Each selected ticket gets its own isolated review execution context
- Ticket prompts and review artifacts do not bleed across tickets
- The epic review run records per-ticket status so users can tell what launched and what finished/failed
- The design supports future parallel execution cleanly, even if the first implementation executes sequentially under the hood

## Notes
Do not create one giant mixed-context review prompt for multiple tickets.

userStories.title: Add regression coverage for epic-scoped focused review runs
acceptanceCriteria: Tests verify that review mode uses a different prompt/launch path than implementation mode
Tests verify that steering prompt text is included in the review launch payload
Tests verify that epic review run records are created and linked to affected tickets
Tests verify that prior ticket comments/findings are preserved
The test suite covers single-ticket and multi-ticket review launch behavior at the appropriate level
userStories.description:
Cover the new review-launch path and history-preserving behavior with regression tests.

## Goal
Prevent the new review flow from silently falling back to the generic Ralph implementation path or breaking existing audit/history behavior.

## Scope
- Add tests for review-mode prompt generation and launch payloads
- Add tests for epic review run record creation and ticket linkage
- Add UI tests for ticket selection and steering prompt submission
- Add coverage that existing ticket comments/findings/history remain visible and unchanged

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests verify that review mode uses a different prompt/launch path than implementation mode
- Tests verify that steering prompt text is included in the review launch payload
- Tests verify that epic review run records are created and linked to affected tickets
- Tests verify that prior ticket comments/findings are preserved
- The test suite covers single-ticket and multi-ticket review launch behavior at the appropriate level

## Notes
Focus on user-visible outcomes and workflow guarantees, not just internal helper coverage.

techStack: Database: SQLite with better-sqlite3, Drizzle ORM
State: TanStack Query for server state

dos: Use Drizzle ORM: `db.select().from(tickets)`
Use typed schema imports: `import { tickets } from "../lib/schema"`
Use `eq()`, `and()`, `sql` from drizzle-orm for conditions
Use transactions for multi-table operations: `db.transaction(() => {...})`
Use `.get()` for single row, `.all()` for multiple
donts: Raw SQL strings: `db.run("SELECT * FROM tickets")`
String-based table names
String concatenation for WHERE clauses
Multiple independent queries that should be atomic
Assume query returns what you expect without checking

dos: Use `useQuery` with `queryKeys` for data fetching
Use `useMutation` with `onSuccess` for state changes
Invalidate queries after mutations: `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: queryKeys.tickets })`
Use centralized `queryKeys` object from `src/lib/hooks.ts`
Create custom hooks in `src/lib/hooks.ts` for reusable query logic
donts: `useState` + `useEffect` for fetched data
Direct API calls without proper cache invalidation
Manual cache manipulation or refetch after every change
Hardcoded query key strings throughout components
Duplicate query setup in multiple components

dos: Use Tailwind classes for static styles: `className="p-4 bg-slate-800"`
Use CSS variables for theming: `var(--bg-primary)`, `var(--shadow-modal)`
Reserve inline styles for dynamic/computed values: `style={{ width: `${percent}%` }}`
Define shared style constants at module level for referential stability
Use `React.CSSProperties` type for inline style objects when needed
donts: Inline style objects for single-use styles
Hardcoded colors that don't adapt to themes
Mix Tailwind and inline styles inconsistently
Create new objects inside components that are reused
Untyped style objects that miss IDE autocomplete

dos: Use `createServerFn({ method: "GET" })` for reads, `"POST"` for writes
Use `.inputValidator()` for type-safe input handling
Return typed data directly: `return db.select().from(tickets).all()`
Use `ensureExists()` for required lookups: `ensureExists(project, "Project")`
Import from `@tanstack/react-start` (not `@tanstack/react-start/server`)
donts: Mix GET/POST inconsistently
Access raw input without validation
Wrap in unnecessary response objects
Return null and let caller handle missing data
Wrong import path

dos: Use Zod schemas for input validation: `{ ticketId: z.string() }`
Return structured `{ content: [{ type: "text", text: ... }] }` format
Set `isError: true` for error responses
Use `log.info()` / `log.error()` from `../lib/logging.js`
Include helpful error messages: `"Project not found. Use list_projects to see available."`
donts: Trust input without validation
Return plain strings
Return error text without the error flag
`console.log` in MCP server code
Generic "Not found" errors

dos: Test user behavior: what users see, click, and experience
Integration tests for workflows: test components together
Real database fixtures with actual schema
Tests that fail when user-facing behavior breaks
Ask: "Does this test catch bugs users would encounter?"
donts: Test implementation details, internal state, or private methods
Unit tests for every function
Excessive mocking of internals
Tests that break on refactoring internals
Chase 100% code coverage as a goal

verificationSteps: Run `pnpm type-check` - must pass with no errors
Run `pnpm lint` - must pass with no errors
Run `pnpm test` - all tests must pass
Added tests for new functionality (ONLY tests that verify real user behavior - see Testing Philosophy above)
Used typed error classes (not generic `Error`)
Used Drizzle ORM (not raw SQL) - see DO/DON'T table above
Followed existing patterns from DO/DON'T tables
No hardcoded values that should be configurable
Existing tests still pass
No regressions in related functionality
Updated tests if behavior changed
Did not break backward compatibility (unless explicitly requested)
Manually verified in browser at `localhost:4242`
Checked responsive layout
Verified TanStack Query invalidates and updates correctly
Accessibility: keyboard navigation works, proper ARIA labels
Migration file created via `pnpm db:generate`
Migration tested with `pnpm db:migrate`
Backup tested if schema changed (use `pnpm brain-dump backup` then test restore)
Updated `src/lib/schema.ts` with proper types and constraints
Tested tool via Claude Code integration
Verified error responses are informative (see DO/DON'T table)
Updated tool documentation if interface changed
Added Zod schema for input validation
All acceptance criteria from ticket met
Work summary added via `comment` tool, `action: "add"` (for Ralph sessions)
Session completed with appropriate outcome (for Ralph sessions)
Committed with proper message format: `feat(<ticket-id>): <description>`

epicDescription:
Add a new review orchestration layer at the epic level without replacing any existing ticket-level review history, findings, comments, demo steps, or human review flow.

## Problem
The current "Review with Ralph" behavior is effectively a generic Ralph relaunch. That does not match the UX implied by an epic review action, and it does not let users steer the AI toward a specific review focus.

## Vision
Users can launch a focused review from an epic, pick one or more tickets, and optionally provide freeform steering instructions such as "focus on auth edge cases and silent failures" or "prioritize UX regressions and missing loading states".

This epic-level review run should:
- act as orchestration and audit context
- preserve all existing ticket-level findings/comments/history
- add ticket-scoped review actions on top of the current workflow
- support future parallel execution across multiple selected tickets

## Key product decisions
- Epic-level review runs are orchestration records, not replacements for ticket findings
- Ticket-level findings/comments remain the source of truth for actionable review output
- A freeform review steering prompt is additive guidance, not permission to bypass workflow rules
- The first version should support one or more selected tickets and be designed so each ticket can run in isolation

## Scope
1. Add epic review run records that store selected tickets, steering prompt, provider/mode, status, and aggregate summary
2. Add a dedicated review launch mode/prompt instead of reusing the generic Ralph implementation prompt
3. Add epic UI for selecting tickets and entering a steering prompt
4. Ensure review results are written to ticket comments/findings while linking back to the epic review run for traceability
5. Support launching review work across multiple selected tickets with clean status visibility

## Non-goals
- Replacing existing ticket comments/findings/demo scripts/human review history
- Inventing a generic arbitrary skill runner in this first pass
- Forcing all review data to live only at the epic level

## Success criteria
- Epic page can start a focused review run with selected tickets and a freeform steering prompt
- Review runs are documented at the epic level and linked to the affected tickets
- Ticket-level review workflow still works and remains fully auditable
- The launch prompt for review mode is clearly different from normal implementation mode
- Existing history remains intact and visible
